model_narrative: We plan a single round trip that starts at a home depot, visits every city exactly once, and returns to the depot. For each ordered pair of cities we decide whether the trip travels directly from the first to the second, and we track the order in which cities are visited along the route. Each direct leg between two cities carries a known travel cost. The objective is to choose the route that makes the total travel cost over all legs taken as small as possible.

sets.name: I
sets.doc: the cities to be visited on the tour; the first member in order is the depot where the tour starts and ends

params.name: c
params.doc: the travel cost of going directly from the first city to the second city of the pair, indexed over ordered pairs of cities

params.name: n
params.doc: the number of cities, equal to the count of members of the city set; used as the big constant in the ordering constraints

vars.name: x
vars.doc: takes the value one when the tour travels directly from the first city of the pair to the second city of the pair, and zero otherwise; the diagonal self-pairs are fixed to zero

vars.name: p
vars.doc: the position of each city along the tour, ranging from zero up to the number of cities minus one; the depot is fixed at position zero

constraints.name: x[i,
constraints.intent: Every city must be departed from exactly once. For each city, exactly one direct leg of the trip leaves that city and heads to some city.

constraints.name: x[i,
constraints.intent: Every city must be arrived at exactly once. For each city, exactly one direct leg of the trip enters that city coming from some city.

constraints.name: depot
constraints.intent: To rule out disconnected sub-loops, the visiting positions of the cities must stay consistent with the legs that are actually taken. For each ordered pair of distinct cities that does not involve the depot, whenever the trip travels directly from the first city to the second, the second city must come later in the visiting order than the first. This is enforced with the standard ordering inequality that stays slack when that leg is not taken.

query: There's a proposal on the table to have the truck reach the remote eastern pair, cities 3 and 14, directly off the northern depot cities 4 and 5 instead of looping in from the central hub. We'd want at least one leg running straight from either of the northern depots into either of those two eastern cities. Put that requirement in and walk me through the resulting tour distance.
expected_pyomo: model.corridor_in = Constraint(expr=sum(model.x[i, j] for i in ['i4', 'i5'] for j in ['i3', 'i14']) >= 1)

query: We're looking at a plan that keeps the truck from short-hopping between the central hub cities 1, 2, 10, 11, 12 and 13. The idea is that none of the legs should run from one of those six hub cities straight to another one of them, so every departure from the hub heads out to a different region. Plug that in and tell me what the full tour ends up costing.
expected_pyomo: model.cap_central = Constraint(expr=sum(model.x[i, j] for i in ['i1', 'i2', 'i10', 'i11', 'i12', 'i13'] for j in ['i1', 'i2', 'i10', 'i11', 'i12', 'i13'] if i != j) <= 0)

query: Engineering is pitching a balance rule between the two depot rings. They want the number of legs the truck runs internally among the southern depot cities 6, 7, 15 and 16 to stay at least two below the number it runs internally among the central hub cities 1, 2, 10, 11, 12 and 13. What does the optimizer return for the tour distance under that rule?
expected_pyomo: model.cluster_balance = Constraint(expr=sum(model.x[i, j] for i in ['i6', 'i7', 'i15', 'i16'] for j in ['i6', 'i7', 'i15', 'i16'] if i != j) <= sum(model.x[i, j] for i in ['i1', 'i2', 'i10', 'i11', 'i12', 'i13'] for j in ['i1', 'i2', 'i10', 'i11', 'i12', 'i13'] if i != j) - 2)

query: Let's try a routing rule for the southern depot cluster, cities 6, 7, 15 and 16. From each of those four cities, the next leg has to leave the cluster entirely rather than hop to another city inside it. Apply that to every one of the four and tell me the resulting tour distance.

query: The board is pitching a two-part change to how the eastern pair gets visited. First, drop the direct shuttle between cities 3 and 14 in both directions so the truck can't just hop between them. Second, require at least one leg to come into the eastern pair straight from the warehouse cluster, cities 8, 9 or 17. Put both pieces in together and walk me through the new tour distance.
expected_pyomo:
model.split_pair = Constraint(expr=model.x['i3', 'i14'] + model.x['i14', 'i3'] <= 0)
model.east_from_warehouse = Constraint(expr=sum(model.x[i, j] for i in ['i8', 'i9', 'i17'] for j in ['i3', 'i14']) >= 1)

query: The plan slips into the eastern pair, cities 3 and 14, through the cheap central hub and then ducks straight back out on the free 3-to-14 shuttle, so the southern depot cities 6, 7, 15 and 16 never actually feed that pair. Shouldn't at least one of those southern depots be the one running a leg into city 3, instead of the truck leaning on that little 3-to-14 hop to escape? Wouldn't tying the entry to a depot and closing off that shuttle tidy the loop? What's the model seeing that keeps it parked on the cheap hand-off?
expected_pyomo:
model.south_into_i3 = Constraint(expr=sum(model.x[i, 'i3'] for i in ['i6', 'i7', 'i15', 'i16']) >= 1)
model.no_free_east_exit = Constraint(expr=model.x['i3', 'i14'] <= 0)

query: The southern depot cities 6 and 7 sit right next to the eastern pair, cities 3 and 14, yet the plan leaves both of them with no leg into that pair at all — they hand off elsewhere and the eastern pair gets fed from the central hub instead. Shouldn't each of those two depots be made to run a leg into the eastern pair, either into city 3 or into city 14? Wouldn't pinning every one of them to the pair clean up the routing? What's the trade-off that keeps the model steering both depots away from it?

query: The warehouse cluster, cities 8, 9 and 17, sits a short hop from city 3, but the plan never lets any of those three be the one that brings the truck into city 3 — it always slides in off the central hub instead. Shouldn't at least one leg into city 3 come straight out of the warehouse cluster? Couldn't we just require that entry to be handed off by one of those three? What's keeping the model from making that move?
